text: Case ID: CASE-2024-001. Patient: 58-year-old male. Chief complaint: Sudden crushing chest pain radiating to left arm, started 2 hours ago. Associated symptoms: Diaphoresis, shortness of breath, nausea. Past medical history: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, 30 pack-year smoking history. Current medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily, atorvastatin 40mg daily. Vitals: BP 145/90 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 37.1°C. Physical exam: Anxious, diaphoretic, lungs clear, heart regular rhythm with no murmurs. ECG: ST elevation 3mm in leads II, III, aVF (inferior STEMI). Labs: Troponin I 5.2 ng/mL (elevated 5x normal), CK-MB elevated. Diagnosis: Acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (inferior wall). Management: Aspirin 325mg chewed, clopidogrel 600mg loading dose, heparin bolus and infusion, emergent cardiac catheterization with PCI to RCA showing 95% stenosis, drug-eluting stent placed. Outcome: Successful revascularization, transferred to CCU, started on dual antiplatelet therapy, beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, high-intensity statin. Discharged day 5 with cardiac rehabilitation referral.

Case ID: CASE-2024-002. Patient: 72-year-old female. Chief complaint: Fever, confusion, decreased urine output for 2 days. History: Type 2 diabetes, recurrent UTIs, recent antibiotic use (ciprofloxacin completed 1 week ago). Vitals: BP 88/50 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, RR 28/min, SpO2 91% on room air, Temp 39.2°C. Physical exam: Altered mental status (GCS 13), dry mucous membranes, delayed capillary refill, suprapubic tenderness, no costovertebral angle tenderness. Labs: WBC 18,000/μL with left shift, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (baseline 0.9), procalcitonin elevated. Urinalysis: pyuria, bacteriuria, nitrite positive. Blood cultures: pending (later positive for E. coli ESBL). qSOFA score: 3/3 (altered mental status, SBP <100, RR ≥22). Diagnosis: Urosepsis with septic shock, acute kidney injury. Management: Immediate fluid resuscitation with 2L crystalloid, norepinephrine infusion started for persistent hypotension (MAP goal ≥65), empiric meropenem 1g IV q8h (ESBL coverage), ICU admission. Outcome: Hemodynamically stabilized after 48 hours, vasopressors weaned off day 3, antibiotics de-escalated based on sensitivities, completed 10-day course, creatinine returned to baseline, discharged to rehabilitation facility.

Case ID: CASE-2024-003. Patient: 45-year-old male. Chief complaint: Sudden severe headache 'worst headache of my life', started 3 hours ago while exercising. Associated symptoms: Photophobia, neck stiffness, nausea with one episode of vomiting. No trauma. Past medical history: Hypertension (poorly controlled). Vitals: BP 180/105 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.0°C. Neurological exam: Alert and oriented x3, GCS 15, photophobia present, nuchal rigidity present, no focal neurological deficits, Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs positive. Non-contrast CT head: Hyperdense material in basal cisterns and sylvian fissures consistent with subarachnoid hemorrhage, no hydrocephalus. CT angiography: 7mm anterior communicating artery aneurysm. Diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (Hunt-Hess grade II, Fisher grade 3). Management: Neurosurgery consultation, ICU admission, nimodipine 60mg PO q4h started for vasospasm prevention, blood pressure control with labetalol (target SBP 140-160 mmHg), seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam, endovascular coiling performed on day 2 without complications. Outcome: Good recovery, no delayed cerebral ischemia, discharged day 14 to rehabilitation, modified Rankin Scale score 1 at 3-month follow-up.

Case ID: CASE-2024-004. Patient: 68-year-old female. Chief complaint: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and slurred speech, witnessed onset 90 minutes ago. Past medical history: Atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated due to fall risk), hypertension, prior TIA 2 years ago. Medications: Metoprolol, amlodipine, aspirin 81mg. Vitals: BP 165/92 mmHg, HR 110 bpm irregular, RR 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air. Neurological exam: Alert, right facial droop, dysarthria, right arm drift (cannot overcome gravity), right leg weakness 3/5 strength, sensory intact, NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS) score 8. Non-contrast CT head: No acute hemorrhage, no early ischemic changes, ASPECTS score 10. CT angiography: Left MCA M1 segment occlusion. Diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke - left MCA territory with large vessel occlusion. Management: Stroke code activated, IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg administered at 105 minutes from symptom onset (within 4.5-hour window), mechanical thrombectomy performed at 3 hours with successful recanalization (TICI 2b). Post-procedure: ICU monitoring, blood pressure management, started on anticoagulation with apixaban for atrial fibrillation after 24-hour repeat CT showed no hemorrhagic transformation. Outcome: Significant improvement, NIHSS 2 at discharge (mild residual right hand weakness), discharged day 7 to acute rehabilitation, independent in ADLs at 90 days.

Case ID: CASE-2024-005. Patient: 35-year-old female. Chief complaint: Severe shortness of breath, wheezing, unable to speak in full sentences. History: Known asthma since childhood, multiple previous ED visits, one prior ICU admission for asthma. Recent upper respiratory infection, ran out of inhaled corticosteroid 1 week ago. Vitals: BP 135/85 mmHg, HR 125 bpm, RR 32/min, SpO2 88% on room air. Physical exam: Sitting upright, tripod position, using accessory muscles, speaking in 2-3 word sentences, diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally, prolonged expiratory phase. Peak flow: 110 L/min (predicted 400 L/min, 27% of predicted). ABG: pH 7.32, pCO2 48 mmHg, pO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 24 mEq/L (respiratory acidosis with hypoxemia). Diagnosis: Severe acute asthma exacerbation, near-fatal asthma. Management: High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask (SpO2 target 94-98%), continuous nebulized albuterol 10mg/hr, ipratropium bromide 500mcg nebulized q20min x3, IV methylprednisolone 125mg, IV magnesium sulfate 2g over 20 minutes. Admitted to ICU for close monitoring. Outcome: Gradual improvement over 6 hours, transitioned to intermittent albuterol nebulizers, switched to oral prednisone 60mg daily, peak flow improved to 280 L/min by day 2. Discharged day 3 with fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler, prednisone taper, albuterol rescue inhaler, asthma action plan, and pulmonology follow-up in 1 week.

Case ID: CASE-2024-006. Patient: 55-year-old male. Chief complaint: Large volume hematemesis (bright red blood), two episodes in past hour, feeling lightheaded. History: Chronic alcohol use disorder, known cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B), prior variceal bleeding 3 years ago with banding. Medications: Propranolol, spironolactone, furosemide. Vitals: BP 92/58 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, RR 22/min, SpO2 94% on room air, Temp 36.8°C. Physical exam: Pale, diaphoretic, jaundiced, spider angiomata, ascites present, splenomegaly, altered but arousable. Labs: Hgb 7.2 g/dL (baseline 11), platelet count 62,000/μL, INR 2.1, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, albumin 2.8 g/dL, total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL. Glasgow-Blatchford score: 12 (high risk). Diagnosis: Acute upper GI bleeding, likely esophageal variceal hemorrhage in setting of cirrhosis. Management: Two large-bore IVs established, aggressive crystalloid resuscitation, transfused 2 units PRBCs (restrictive strategy, target Hgb 7-9), octreotide bolus 50mcg IV then infusion 50mcg/hr, ceftriaxone 1g IV for SBP prophylaxis, pantoprazole 80mg IV bolus then 8mg/hr infusion, FFP and vitamin K for coagulopathy correction. Emergent upper endoscopy: Large esophageal varices with active bleeding, successful band ligation of 4 varices. Outcome: Bleeding controlled, hemodynamically stable, continued octreotide for 5 days, transitioned to oral propranolol, discharged day 7 with GI follow-up for repeat endoscopy in 2 weeks and transplant hepatology referral.

Case ID: CASE-2024-007. Patient: 28-year-old female. Chief complaint: Facial swelling, difficulty breathing, throat tightness, started 10 minutes after eating at restaurant (shrimp dish). History: Known shellfish allergy (previous mild reactions with hives only), carries epinephrine auto-injector but did not use it. Vitals: BP 85/50 mmHg, HR 135 bpm, RR 28/min, SpO2 89% on room air, Temp 37.2°C. Physical exam: Anxious, diffuse urticaria and angioedema of face/lips/tongue, audible stridor, bilateral wheezing on auscultation, weak pulse. Diagnosis: Anaphylaxis (severe, life-threatening). Management: Immediate IM epinephrine 0.5mg (1:1000) into right thigh, patient placed supine with legs elevated, high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask, established two large-bore IVs, rapid 1L normal saline bolus for hypotension. Repeat epinephrine 0.5mg IM at 5 minutes due to persistent hypotension and respiratory distress. Adjunctive therapy: diphenhydramine 50mg IV, ranitidine 50mg IV, methylprednisolone 125mg IV, nebulized albuterol for bronchospasm. Outcome: Gradual improvement over 30 minutes, BP stabilized at 110/70, stridor resolved, wheezing improved. Observed in ED for 6 hours for potential biphasic reaction (none occurred). Discharged with epinephrine auto-injector x2, strict shellfish avoidance instructions, prednisone 60mg daily x5 days, allergist referral. Patient educated on immediate epinephrine use for future reactions.

Case ID: CASE-2024-008. Patient: 19-year-old male with type 1 diabetes. Chief complaint: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, increased urination and thirst for 2 days. History: Type 1 diabetes diagnosed age 12, poor compliance with insulin regimen, ran out of insulin 4 days ago. Vitals: BP 105/68 mmHg, HR 115 bpm, RR 28/min (Kussmaul respirations), SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.4°C. Physical exam: Lethargic but arousable, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, fruity breath odor, diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding, tachycardia. Labs: Glucose 485 mg/dL, pH 7.18 (severe acidosis), bicarbonate 9 mEq/L, anion gap 28, beta-hydroxybutyrate 6.2 mmol/L (elevated), potassium 5.4 mEq/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (prerenal azotemia), urinalysis with 4+ glucose and 3+ ketones. Diagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), severe. Management: Aggressive fluid resuscitation with 0.9% normal saline 1.5L in first hour, then 500 mL/hr. IV regular insulin: 10 units bolus, then continuous infusion at 10 units/hr (0.1 units/kg/hr). Potassium monitoring: K+ dropped to 3.8 after 2 hours, added 40 mEq KCl to each liter of IV fluid. When glucose reached 250 mg/dL, switched to D5 0.45% saline and reduced insulin to 5 units/hr. Outcome: DKA resolved after 14 hours (pH 7.32, bicarbonate 18, anion gap 11, glucose 180). Transitioned to subcutaneous insulin regimen (basal-bolus), diabetes education provided, endocrinology follow-up arranged, discharged day 3.
